old_claim.note: Isotope Separation provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Isotope separation and enrichment matter for many nuclear fuel cycles and weapons programs, but fission itself and some reactors do not require it as a universal prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: EIA nuclear fuel cycle page describing uranium fuel, U-235, and fuel-cycle processing for nuclear power plants.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source places isotope handling in the nuclear fuel-cycle context rather than as a universal prerequisite for the physical fission process.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a scaling/fuel-cycle edge because enriched fuel is important for many applications, while fission discovery and some reactor designs are broader than isotope separation.

ontology_before: The edge implied isotope separation was a general enabling prerequisite for nuclear fission.
ontology_after: The edge is limited to commercial and fuel-cycle scaling dependencies.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant added in 2026-06-07-energy-edge-source-scope.json to lock the corrected Energy Systems edge semantics.
would_reject_if: A more specific primary or textbook source showed the original dependency type was globally required for the scoped technology.
The dependent node were narrowed to a later industrial product where the old prerequisite is truly mandatory.
A future source-check splits the node and moves this edge onto a more precise downstream technology.
short_reason: Isotope separation belongs to fuel-cycle scaling, not fission itself.
